meta.title: Data Abstraction
meta.intro: You warmed these up on paper in Trace the List and One Name for Many. Now build and run them for real with the weather station's daily-high temperatures — the problems get harder as you go, ending with one you build from scratch. Remember: the exam sheet counts from index 1, but Python counts from index 0, so translate every position down by one when you code. Predict the output first, then check yourself.

problems.prompt: Create a list named temps holding the daily highs 68, 72, 75, and 70, then print exactly the FIRST reading: 68
problems.promptHtml: Create a list named temps holding the daily highs 68, 72, 75, 70, then print exactly the FIRST reading: 68

starter.python:
# make a list called temps holding 68, 72, 75, 70
# print the FIRST reading (exam sheet index 1 = Python index 0)
starter.javascript:
// make a list called temps holding 68, 72, 75, 70
// print the FIRST reading (exam sheet index 1 = Python index 0)

problems.hint: Build the list with square brackets and commas. The exam sheet's first element is temps[1]; in Python and JavaScript that same first element is temps[0].

problems.prompt: Start with temps holding 68, 72, 75, 70. Append a new reading, 81, to the end, then print how many readings are now stored.
problems.promptHtml: Start with temps holding 68, 72, 75, 70. Append a new reading, 81, to the end, then print how many readings are now stored.

starter.python:
temps = [68, 72, 75, 70]
# append 81 to the end of temps
# print how many readings are now in the list

problems.hint: Appending adds one element to the end, so the length grows by 1. Use the length function/property to count the elements after appending — do not hard-code 5.

problems.prompt: The list temps holds 68, 72, 75, 70. Print the THIRD reading and the LAST reading on one line, separated by a space, like: 75 70
problems.promptHtml: The list temps holds 68, 72, 75, 70. Print the THIRD reading and the LAST reading on one line, separated by a space, like: 75 70

starter.python:
temps = [68, 72, 75, 70]
# print the THIRD reading and the LAST reading, separated by a space
# exam index 3 = Python index 2; the last element is at index len(temps)-1

problems.hint: The exam sheet's third element temps[3] is Python's temps[2]. The last element is at index len(temps)-1, which reaches the end no matter how long the list is.

problems.prompt: Build the abstraction from scratch: create a list named highs holding 90, 85, and 88, append 92, then print the FIRST and LAST readings separated by a space, like: 90 92
problems.promptHtml: Build the abstraction from scratch: create a list named highs holding 90, 85, 88, append 92, then print the FIRST and LAST readings separated by a space, like: 90 92

starter.python:
# create a list named highs holding 90, 85, 88
# append 92 to the end
# print the FIRST reading and the LAST reading, separated by a space

problems.hint: After appending, the last element is at index len(highs)-1 (exam sheet index LENGTH(highs)). The first element is highs[0] in Python (exam sheet highs[1]). Join the two with a space.
